messages.content: can you paraphrase (Machine Learning models (ML) were developed to help in detecting the COVID-19 patients’ need for the ICU and the estimated duration of their stay. Four ML algorithms were utilized: Random Forest (RF), Gradient Boosting (GB), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), and Ensemble models were trained and validated on a dataset of 895 COVID-19 patients admitted to King Fahad University Hospital in the eastern province of Saudi Arabia. The conducted experiments show that the Length of Stay (LOS) in the ICU can be predicted with the highest accuracy by applying the RF model for prediction, as the achieved accuracy was 94.16%.)

Machine Learning models were used to identify COVID-19 patients who required ICU care and determine how long they would stay. Four ML algorithms were employed: Random Forest (RF), Gradient Boosting (GB), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), and Ensemble models. These models were trained and tested on a dataset consisting of 895 COVID-19 patients who were admitted to King Fahad University Hospital in Saudi Arabia. The results of the experiments demonstrated that the RF model was the most accurate in predicting the Length of Stay (LOS) in the ICU, achieving an accuracy of 94.16%.

can you paraphrase (The aim of Omar's paper is to apply ML to predict the acute patient's Length of Stay in a hospital setting by implementing and comparing two Machine Learning (ML) methods to predict the LOS in a hospital setting, the Random Forest (RF) and the Gradient Boosting model (GB), using an open-source available dataset. These data are been firstly preprocessed by combining data transformation, data standardization, and data codification. Then, the RF and the GB were carried out, with a phase of hyperparameters tuning until setting optimal coefficients. Finally, the Mean Square Error (MAE), the R-squared (R2), and the Adjusted R-squared (Adjusted R2) metrics are selected to evaluate the model with parameters. Both models has R2 greater than 0.92 and MAE lower than 0.45)

Omar's paper aims to utilize Machine Learning (ML) to predict the Length of Stay (LOS) for acute patients in a hospital setting. The study compares two ML methods, namely Random Forest (RF) and Gradient Boosting (GB), using an available open-source dataset. The data is initially preprocessed through a combination of data transformation, standardization, and codification. The RF and GB methods are then implemented, with hyperparameter tuning to optimize the coefficients. Evaluation of the models is done using metrics such as Mean Square Error (MAE), R-squared (R2), and Adjusted R-squared (Adjusted R2). Both models yield an R2 value greater than 0.92 and an MAE lower than 0.45.

can you paraphrase (Omar proposed a framework for predicting patient LOS in the ICU using different machine learning (ML) techniques. Unlike most of the previous studies, this study relies on general medical features collected on patient admission regardless of the patient's diagnosis. This provides a broad scope and covers all patients making this approach general and easy to use. The prediction accuracy of the proposed approach was recorded to be very high and different for each ML technique. For example, the best prediction accuracy was achieved by fuzzy with an accuracy reaching 92%, while the classification tree managed to achieve a prediction accuracy of 90% coming in the second place.)

Omar put forth a framework that utilizes various machine learning (ML) techniques to predict the Length of Stay (LOS) for ICU patients. In contrast to previous studies, this approach focuses on using general medical features collected upon patient admission, regardless of their diagnosis. This broadens the scope and makes the approach applicable to all patients, making it user-friendly. The accuracy of the predictions achieved by this approach was notably high and varied across different ML techniques. For instance, the fuzzy technique demonstrated the highest accuracy at 92%, while the classification tree technique came in second place with a prediction accuracy of 90%.

can you paraphrase  (Omar's paper aimed to predict the length of stay for patients with diabetes by applying machine learning techniques to clinical data available during the first 8 hours of ICU admissions. Two prediction tasks, the number of days in the ICU and whether an ICU stay is long or short distinguished by the threshold of 10 days, were explored. The neural network model achieved the best performance in predicting the number of days in the ICU with an R2 value of 0.3969 and a mean absolute error of 1.94 days. The gradient boosting model is the best one to classify long and short ICU stays with an accuracy of 0.8214. The results demonstrate that these two models are promising to estimate the length of stay at an early stage of ICU admissions for patients with diabetes.)

Omar's paper aimed to utilize machine learning techniques on clinical data gathered within the first 8 hours of ICU admissions to predict the length of stay for patients with diabetes. Two separate prediction tasks were explored: determining the number of days a patient would spend in the ICU and classifying whether their ICU stay would be long or short (with a threshold of 10 days). The neural network model demonstrated the highest performance in predicting the number of days in the ICU, achieving an R2 value of 0.3969 and a mean absolute error of 1.94 days. On the other hand, the gradient boosting model proved to be the most accurate in classifying long and short ICU stays, with an accuracy of 0.8214. These results highlight the potential of these two models in estimating the length of stay during the early stages of ICU admissions for patients with diabetes.

can you paraphrase (Omar developed machine learning models to predict the hospital length of stay from open healthcare data. The length of stay is an important metric of the performance of healthcare systems. It can be used to drive cost efficiencies and allocate resources. We analyzed de-identified patient data from the New York State SPARCS (statewide Planning and research cooperative system), consisting of 2.3 million patient records. We investigated multiple model categories consisting of regression, decision trees, random forests, and XGBoost. The most important features we identified were the diagnostic-related group and the severity of the illness code. The best regression model was XGBoost, with an R2 value of 0.44.)

Omar employed machine learning models to forecast the length of stay in hospitals using publicly available healthcare data. The length of stay serves as a crucial metric for evaluating healthcare system effectiveness, aiding in cost efficiencies and resource allocation. The study involved analyzing de-identified patient data sourced from the New York State SPARCS (Statewide Planning and Research Cooperative System), comprising 2.3 million patient records. Various model categories, including regression, decision trees, random forests, and XGBoost, were explored. Notably, the key features discovered were the diagnostic-related group and the severity of the illness code. XGBoost emerged as the top-performing regression model, achieving an R2 value of 0.44.

can you paraphrase (Omar developed a machine learning approach for analyzing patient data and building a reliable prediction model incorporating the Random Forest Regression Model to estimate the length of a patient’s stay in the hospital. The data utilized in this study is a hospital discharge dataset containing records with various types of patient information. Two types of feature selection methods (PCA and Chi-square) and interquartile range-based outlier elimination strategy have also been employed in the model for efficient prediction. The best model is a Random Forest with an MAE of 1.)

Omar devised a machine learning method that involves analyzing patient data and constructing a robust prediction model using the Random Forest Regression Model to estimate the length of hospital stays. The study employed a hospital discharge dataset encompassing diverse patient information. To enhance prediction efficiency, two feature selection methods (PCA and Chi-square) and an interquartile range-based outlier elimination strategy were incorporated into the model. The Random Forest model emerged as the most effective, achieving a mean absolute error (MAE) of 1.

can you paraphrase (Omar applied machine learning (ML) methods to predict the Length of Stay in an Intensive Care Unit (LOS-ICU) based on preoperative factors. To optimize the capacity of the ICU in the surgery department, the prediction of a long stay (more than 2 days) can support the clinical decision-making on accepting or delaying a patient intervention, considering the ICU occupancy. A database with records from 7364 patients that were operated on in the Cardiothoracic surgery department of a public Portuguese hospital was used as the basis of ML algorithms training. Regarding the risk of the patients being in the group of long LOS-ICU, Omar compared five machine learning algorithms including Gradient Boosting, Random Forest, Support Vector Machine (SVM), AdaBoost, and Logistic Regression. Random forest achieved the highest accuracy of 76%. )

Omar employed machine learning (ML) techniques to predict the Length of Stay in an Intensive Care Unit (LOS-ICU) based on preoperative factors. The aim was to optimize ICU capacity in the surgery department by predicting long stays (over 2 days). This prediction can assist clinical decision-making in determining whether to accept or delay a patient's intervention, taking into account ICU occupancy. The study utilized a database consisting of 7,364 patient records from the Cardiothoracic surgery department of a public Portuguese hospital for training ML algorithms. To assess the risk of patients falling into the long LOS-ICU group, Omar compared five ML algorithms: Gradient Boosting, Random Forest, Support Vector Machine (SVM), AdaBoost, and Logistic Regression. Random Forest exhibited the highest accuracy, achieving 76%.

can you paraphrase (Omar developed the application of machine learning-based prediction of a Healthcare Cost and Utilization Project (HCUP) dataset and reported on the performance of various developed predictive models. Via only utilizing administrative data and minimal clinical data available near the time of admission/birth, we are able to demonstrate high-performing models for predicting the newborn prolonged length of stay. Random Forest achieved the best ROC of 0.877.)

Omar focused on utilizing machine learning for predicting healthcare costs and utilization using a dataset from the Healthcare Cost and Utilization Project (HCUP). The study evaluated the performance of different predictive models that were developed. By relying solely on administrative data and limited clinical data available around the time of admission or birth, the study demonstrated the effectiveness of these models in accurately predicting prolonged length of stay for newborns. Among the models tested, Random Forest yielded the highest performance with an ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) value of 0.877.

can you paraphrase (Omar assessed the performance improvement for machine learning-based hospital length of stay (LOS) predictions when clinical signs written in the text are accounted for and compared to the traditional approach of solely considering structured information such as age, gender, and major ICD diagnosis. They developed two random forest models and the model using unstructured data had a 75.0% accuracy compared to 74.1% for the model containing structured data. Then they implemented the feature importance for both structured and unstructured data.)

Omar conducted an assessment to determine the impact of incorporating clinical signs mentioned in textual data on the accuracy of machine learning-based predictions for hospital length of stay (LOS). This was compared to the conventional approach of solely considering structured information like age, gender, and major ICD diagnosis. They developed two random forest models, one using unstructured data and the other using structured data. The model utilizing unstructured data achieved an accuracy of 75.0%, whereas the model incorporating structured data demonstrated an accuracy of 74.1%. Feature importance was then implemented for both structured and unstructured data to analyze their respective contributions to the predictions.

paraphrase (Omar explored Machine Learning (ML) models that best predict Prolonged Hospital Length of Stay (PLOS). Used data collected from the French Medico-Administrative Database (PMSI) as a retrospective cohort study of all discharges in the year 2015 from a large university hospital in France (APHM). The study outcomes were LOS transformed into a binary variable (long vs. short LOS). Logistic regression (LR), classification and regression trees (CART), random forest (RF), gradient boosting (GB), and neural networks (NN) were applied to the collected data. Gradient boosting (GB) is the best model with AUC of 0.8101.)

Omar investigated the effectiveness of various Machine Learning (ML) models in predicting Prolonged Hospital Length of Stay (PLOS). The study utilized data obtained from the French Medico-Administrative Database (PMSI) as a retrospective cohort study, specifically focusing on all discharges from a large university hospital in France (APHM) in the year 2015. The primary outcome of interest was the length of stay, which was transformed into a binary variable distinguishing between long and short stays. Different ML algorithms such as Logistic Regression (LR), Classification and Regression Trees (CART), Random Forest (RF), Gradient Boosting (GB), and Neural Networks (NN) were employed to analyze the data. It was observed that Gradient Boosting (GB) yielded the most reliable model, achieving an AUC (Area Under the Curve) score of 0.8101.
